Featured Analysis — with Original Source Pages
Ruhani Khazain Vol. 2 Miracles & Science June 2026

Does the Author Believe in Animal Life on the Moon?

کیا مصنف چاند پر حیوانات کے وجود کا قائل ہے؟

A careful reading of Ruhani Khazain Vol. 2, pp. 276–278 shows that opponents have fundamentally misread the passage. The author cites contemporary philosophical opinion as a rhetorical device — an ilzami argument — not as a personal belief. Original source pages are included for verification.

روحانی خزائن جلد دوم کے صفحات ۲۷۶ تا ۲۷۸ کا بغور مطالعہ ظاہر کرتا ہے کہ مخالفین نے اس عبارت کو بنیادی طور پر غلط سمجھا ہے۔

About This Blogاس بلاگ کے بارے میں

This blog responds to objections against Ahmadiyya beliefs by returning directly to primary sources — reading passages in full context, with original page images included for verification.

یہ بلاگ اعتراضات کا جواب اصل ماخذ کی طرف رجوع کر کے دیتا ہے — اصل صفحات کی تصاویر کے ساتھ۔

Key Principleاہم اصول

"Citing a premise is not the same as owning it. Read the full argument before judging the conclusion."

کسی دلیل کو بیان کرنا اسے اپنا عقیدہ ماننا نہیں ہے۔

Does the Author Believe in Animal Life on the Moon?

کیا مصنف چاند پر حیوانات کے وجود کا قائل ہے؟
The Objection

The author uses the idea of animal life on the moon to support his argument — therefore this must be his personal belief. He is endorsing a false scientific claim as part of his theology.

Original Source Pages — click to enlarge اصل صفحات — بڑا دیکھنے کے لیے کلک کریں
Page 276
Page 277
Page 278 — Key Page
Translation of Key Passage — Page 278 صفحہ ۲۷۸ کا ترجمہ
Natural English Translation

Furthermore, this is not merely a one-time event. Rather, in a hidden manner, the splitting and joining is always occurring in the sun and moon. This is because the philosophy of this era expresses its firm opinion that within the sun and moon there exists a similar population of animals, plants, and other such things, just as they exist on the Earth.

And the splitting and joining of the moon is continually taking place. Because the physical matter of the moon is always connected to the physical matter of the living creatures of the Earth — their movement is always present in it — and from this it is established as proven that the splitting of the moon is always necessary.

اصل اردو عبارت

اور یہ محض ایک مرتبہ نہیں بلکہ مخفی طور پر انشقاق و اتصال ہمیشہ سورج وقمر میں جاری ہے۔ کیونکہ اس زمانے کی فلاسفی اپنی مضبوط رائے ظاہر کرتی ہے کہ سورج وقمر میں ایسی ہی آبادی حیوانات ونباتات وغیرہ ہے جیسی زمین پر ہے۔

اور زمین پر امر انشقاق قمری کو ثابت کرنے والا ہے کیونکہ جس کرہ میں حیوانات ونباتات پیدا ہوتے ہیں وہ اسی کرہ کے مادہ لے کر جسم بناتے ہیں۔

Analysis — Why the Objection Fails

The key phrase in the original Urdu is فلاسفی اپنی مضبوط رائے ظاہر کرتی ہے — "philosophy expresses its firm opinion." This is unambiguous attribution language. The author is reporting what the scientists and philosophers of his era believed, not declaring his own position.

The argumentative technique being used here is called ilzami in classical Islamic rhetoric — binding an opponent with their own accepted premises. The author is saying: "You accept far more extraordinary scientific speculation about the moon, yet reject a Quranic miracle — this is inconsistent on your own terms."

This is identical to how a lawyer argues ex concessis — from what the opposing party has already conceded. It does not require the lawyer to personally believe the conceded point. Any honest reading of pages 276–284 as a whole confirms this: the author's entire focus is on defending the Quranic miracle of Shaq al-Qamar against rationalist opponents. The moon-life reference is one supporting point borrowed from the opponent's own worldview.

Opponents who claim this is his personal belief have not read the passage. They have read a decontextualized summary stripped of its rhetorical structure.

Historical Verification — Was This Actually the Scientific Opinion of the Era?

This is where the author's accuracy becomes remarkable. Belief in life on the moon was not fringe speculation in the 19th century — it was the mainstream philosophical and scientific position held by some of the greatest minds of the age.

William Herschel (1738–1822)

Discoverer of Uranus and one of the greatest astronomers in history. He viewed all planets and moons as inhabited and claimed his telescopes revealed forests and cities on the moon. His published and unpublished writings show that belief in a plurality of inhabited worlds at times motivated his research and observations.

Source: Springer — William Herschel and the Moon (peer-reviewed)
Thomas Dick (1838)

Prominent writer on Christianity, philosophy, and astronomy. Wrote that the moon was inhabited by rational creatures whose surface was covered with vegetation similar to Earth, and that telescopes could observe artificial structures built by lunar inhabitants.

Source: Library of Congress — Life on Other Worlds
Adam Clarke — Theologian & Scholar

Eminent Biblical scholar and theologian who wrote that there was "scarcely any doubt now remaining in the philosophical world" that the moon was a habitable globe — citing the most accurate observations with the most powerful telescopes available.

Source: Mormon Research — citing Crowe, The Extraterrestrial Life Debate
The Young Astronomer Textbook (1846)

A standard educational astronomy textbook of the era stated plainly: "It is the general opinion of astronomers that the Moon is inhabited." This was being taught to students as the accepted scientific position.

Source: Library of Congress — Life on Other Worlds

The broader consensus is confirmed by historian Michael J. Crowe, who documented in detail that belief in a plurality of inhabited worlds — including the moon — was widespread among both scientific and religious thinkers of the 18th and 19th centuries. By the end of the 18th century this belief was described as "nearly universal" in scientific circles.

When the author wrote that "the philosophy of this era expresses its firm opinion" about life on celestial bodies, he was therefore making an accurate statement about the scientific consensus of his time. This further confirms that he was citing an external position, not expressing a personal theological claim.

A Notable Observation — The Author's Intellectual Awareness

It is worth pausing to note something remarkable here. The author was writing in 19th century India, yet he was clearly aware of the contemporary European scientific and philosophical debate around life on celestial bodies — a debate being driven by figures like William Herschel and documented in textbooks and popular science writing of the era.

This suggests the author was widely read in the scientific literature of his time, and was engaging with his rationalist opponents not from ignorance but from genuine intellectual familiarity with their own sources. He knew what the philosophers and scientists of his era were saying, and he used that knowledge strategically and accurately.

Far from being an embarrassment, this passage is evidence of the author's intellectual sophistication — and it makes the opponents' misreading of it all the more difficult to excuse.

The author does not claim to believe in animal life on the moon. He accurately cites the dominant scientific and philosophical opinion of his era — held by figures including William Herschel, Thomas Dick, and Adam Clarke — as an ilzami (binding) argument against rationalist opponents. Historical research fully confirms that this was indeed the mainstream view of 19th century science. The original Urdu text makes the attribution explicit.

مصنف نے چاند پر حیوانات کے وجود کو اپنا عقیدہ نہیں کہا۔ انہوں نے اسے اپنے زمانے کی سائنسی رائے کے طور پر بیان کیا — جو ولیم ہرشل اور دیگر علماء کی رائے تھی — تاکہ عقل پرستوں پر الزامی حجت قائم کی جا سکے۔

Primary source: Ruhani Khazain, Vol. 2 (Surma Chashma Arya), pp. 276–278. Historical references: Springer Nature (peer-reviewed), Library of Congress, Michael J. Crowe — The Extraterrestrial Life Debate 1750–1900.
